coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence turn-off chemosensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots for detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yuliang
Han, Qiaorong
Jin, Can
Zhang, Jing
Wang, Bingxiang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, new carbon nanomaterial N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were obtained via a one-step hydrothermal method using isoleucine and citric acid as carbon precursors. TEM results showed that the NCD diameters were not uniform but distributed in the 6–15nm range, with an average value of 10nm. The maximum emission wavelength of the obtained NCDs was 415nm with an excitation wavelength of 370nm. Under optimal conditions, the nanoparticle can be used as sensors and exhibited a ‘turn-off’ fluorescence response to Fe3+, with a good linear relationship of (F/F 0) versus Fe3+ over the concentration range of 0–20μmol/L.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Carbon nanomaterial
Nanoparticle
Sensors
Turn-off
Fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The fluorescence spectra of NCDs (1.0×10−3 M) upon the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50, and 60μM). (b) A linear relationship of (F/F 0) versus the concentration of Fe3+ over the range from 10 to 60μM. (For interpretation of the reference to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence decay curves of NCDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+.
Cartoon illustrations for the synthesis process of NCDs.
The fluorescence intensities of NCDs with 50μM metal ion (λ ex=370nm, silt=5×5).

head.ce:title: A fluorescence turn-off chemosensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots for detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210097, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Biomass Energy and Materials, National Engineering Laboratory for Biomass Chemical Utilization, Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, CAF, Nanjing 210042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Biomass Energy and Materials, National Engineering Laboratory for Biomass Chemical Utilization, Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, CAF

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, new carbon nanomaterial N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were obtained via a one-step hydrothermal method using isoleucine and citric acid as carbon precursors. TEM results showed that the NCD diameters were not uniform but distributed in the 6–15nm range, with an average value of 10nm. The maximum emission wavelength of the obtained NCDs was 415nm with an excitation wavelength of 370nm. Under optimal conditions, the nanoparticle can be used as sensors and exhibited a ‘turn-off’ fluorescence response to Fe3+, with a good linear relationship of (F/F 0) versus Fe3+ over the concentration range of 0–20μmol/L.

ce:section.ce:para: The design and development of fluorescence sensors for the detection of Fe3+ is becoming an important issue because of the vital roles Fe3+ play in environmental, biological, and chemical systems [1–5]. Recently, a large number of fluorescent chemosensors were exploited as useful tools for the detection of Fe3+ owing to their intrinsic sensitivity, selectivity, and capacity for rapid, real-time monitoring [6–10]. Most of these chemosensors were organic dyes with environmental unfriendly, time-consuming, and cytotoxic chemical syntheses [11–14], which restricted their practical performance. Therefore, the development of an efficient low-toxicity, high-efficiency, and stable chemical probe is urgent.

Carbon dots (CDs), as the newest class of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted a great deal of attention for their fascinating properties which include size- and excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, good stability, and easy preparation; they are also nonblinking and photostable [15–20]. Moreover, some shortcomings such as low quantum yields and emission of only blue light hinder their applications. Thus, it is of great significance to take advantage of NCDs through surface modification of CDs to improve their luminescent properties for the effective detection of various metal ions.

Herein, we report a green and facile preparative route to luminescent NCDs by a direct hydrothermal method using isoleucine and citric acid (CA). The NCDs can be used for ‘turn-off’ detection of Fe3+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. The preparation procedures of NCDs are shown in Scheme 1.

Materials: Citric acid monohydrate and isoleucine were purchased from Aladdin chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and were utilized without further purification. All the other chemicals used in this paper were obtained from commercial suppliers and used directly untreated. Inorganic salts MnCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, NaNO3, KNO3, Fe(NO3)3·6H2O, Co(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Cd(NO3)2·2H2O, NiNO3·6H2O, AgNO3 and Hg(NO3)2·0.5H2O were dissolved in double distilled water (100mL) to afford 1.0×10−3 mol/L aqueous solutions. All samples were prepared at room temperature.

Characterization: UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a Varian Cary 50spectrophotometer. The IR spectra of the product were measured by a Nicolet Nexus 670 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscope. The microstructures of NCDs were examined using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which were recorded with a JEOL JEM-2100. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Varian cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. Xe lamp was used as an excitation source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on a PHI 5000 Versa Probe electron spectrometer from ULVAC-PHI.

Preparation of NCDs: Typical preparation of NCDs was described as follows: briefly, CA monohydrate (0.21g, 1.0mmol) and isoleucine (0.28g, 1.0mmol) were dissolved in water (5mL) and heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave at 180°C for 5h. After cooling to room temperature the brown syrup product was neutralized with 1mol/L NaOH solution. The large particles were removed by filtration using a 0.22μm filter membrane and then centrifugated at 12,000rpm for 15min. The obtained NCDs were further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for three days. The blue fluorescent NCDs were obtained.

Principles of fluorescence quenching: Dynamic quenching and static quenching are two basic forms of fluorescence quenching. The following formulas 1 and 2 represent the corresponding Stern–Volmer equation. For Eq. (1) F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of the quencher respectively, K q is the rate constant of dynamic quenching, τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime in the absence of the quenchers; τ is the fluorescence lifetime in the presence of quenchers, and [Q] is the quencher concentration in solution.
(1)
F
0
F
=
τ
0
τ
= 1 +
k
q
τ
0
[ Q ]
(2)
F
0
F
= 1 +
k
SV
[ Q ]

Static quenching occurs as a result of the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between the ground state of fluorophore and quencher in Eq. (2) the K SV is now the association constant K S. While the life-time of the fluorophore is not affected by the static quenching, lifetime measurements are a definitive method to distinguish between static and dynamic quenching as reported [21].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs dispersed in water (Fig. S1) shown two typical absorption peaks at 242 and 343nm, the two peaks are ascribed to π–π⁎ transition of the CC bond and nπ⁎ transition of the CO bond, respectively, which suggests that the honeycomb frame was obtained through hydrothermal procedure.

The structure and composition of NCDs were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S2, the NCDs show a main absorption band of OH at 3430cm−1, CH at 2970 and 2850cm−1, stretching vibration of CO at 1570cm−1, the typical band of the amine CN(III) bond is at 1430cm−1, these results showed that amino acid as nitrogen source doped carbon quantum dots successfully and the NCDs are rich in carboxylic groups, which make it as high water solubility.
From TEM we can find that the NCDs are highly dispersed in aqueous solution and their diameters are not of uniform size distributed in the range of 6–15nm with an average diameter of 10nm ( Fig. 1).

The composition of NCDs was carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. The full scan XPS spectrum of NCDs (Fig. S3a) clearly reveals that carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are present at the surface of NCDs, as shown in Fig. S3b 1.14% of nitrogen and 62.79% of carbon indicate that the NCDs are nitrogen-doped and carbon-rich.

The response of NCDs in the presence of Fe3+ was determined by titration of the NCDs with Fe3+ in phosphate buffer solution (pH=6.8). Upon addition of Fe3+ at a 0–60μM concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the NCD solution was quenched by 93%. The color of the solution also changed from blue to colorless, as detected visually under a 365nm UV lamp ( Fig. 2a). From Job׳s plot analysis, we found a good linear relationship of (F/F 0) versus the concentration of Fe3+ over a range of 0–20μM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.98 (Fig. 2b), which confirmed previous results that Fe3+ interacts with NCDs by chelating with the oxygen functional groups on the surface of NCDs to result in fluorescence quenching. Under the same conditions, the addition of other anions, including Na+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, and Fe2+, resulted in small or no obvious fluorescence changes ( Table 1). These findings validate the high sensitivity and selectivity of the NCDs toward Fe3+.

To further study the mechanism of the quenching process, the fluorescence decays were used to test the process of NCDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ions. Fig. 3 shows the fluorescence decays of NCDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+. Lifetime study shows that before and after introducing Fe3+ to the aqueous solution, the lifetime of NCDs exhibit obvious shift from 5.7ns to 4.5ns which suggests that the mechanism of the quenching process is dynamic quenching.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized for the first time a novel ‘turn-off’ fluorescent probe based on NCDs by the facile, green, one-step hydrothermal treatment of isoleucine and citric acid. The NCDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity for Fe3+ relative to other competitive metal ions such as Na+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, and Fe2+, by reducing the fluorescence emission at 415nm. The fluorescence decay of the NCDs in the presence of Fe3+ revealed that the mechanism of quenching involves complexation and that dynamic quenching dominates over static quenching.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21202101, 31400516) and a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2014.10.168.
